does: This paper introduces UXBench, a user-centric benchmark for evaluating AI assistants on user experience metrics rather than general capability. The benchmark comprises three interconnected tasks—UX Judge for user preference prediction, UX Eval for experience assessment, and UX Recovery for dialogue improvement—with 7,400 test instances derived from over 70,000 real interaction logs from a mainstream Chinese AI assistant. The dataset reflects authentic user distributions across 8 scenarios and 83 domains, capturing diverse failure patterns. The authors evaluate 26 frontier language models, demonstrating that user feedback prediction is a learnable capability where reward models trained on in-the-wild signals achieve well-calibrated accuracy. The work reveals the relationship between model capability improvements and dialogue engagement quality, providing concrete insights into how different models perceive and satisfy user experience.
connects: This work extends prior efforts in AI evaluation beyond standard benchmarks focused on factuality and reasoning toward preference-aligned generation. It builds on reward modeling research that learns from human feedback signals, connecting to work on RLHF and alignment evaluation. The paper addresses gaps in existing benchmarks by grounding evaluation in actual user interactions rather than synthetic or expert-annotated data, extending dialogue evaluation frameworks that typically focus on task completion or coherence. It relates to work on Chinese language understanding and culturally-grounded NLP evaluation. The emphasis on real-world failure modes and distribution shifts connects to recent work on robustness and out-of-distribution evaluation, while the multi-task framing builds on approaches that probe different aspects of model behavior through interconnected evaluation tasks.
enables: This benchmark enables future work in developing user-centric optimization objectives for AI assistants, moving beyond capability-centric training toward engagement-optimized systems. It provides a foundation for training better reward models that capture nuanced user preferences from diverse interaction contexts, facilitating more sophisticated RLHF approaches. The real-world distribution and failure patterns documented in UXBench enable researchers to study generalization across user demographics and regional variations in AI assistant preferences. The dataset supports developing methods for dialogue recovery and failure detection, potentially enabling more robust assistant behavior. The framework opens directions for probing how architectural choices, scaling, and different training methods affect user experience perception, and provides a baseline for evaluating emerging assistants. It also supports research on cross-cultural and multi-language AI evaluation by establishing methodology for mining and structuring real-world user signals.
